text: Biomedica was born about thirty years ago. I was in this field fifteen years before and I was an agent of a company with characteristics quite similar to what Biomedica is today. I started in Rome, to be exact. There have been a couple of opportunities, in particular a very simple one, where they invented a catheter with only one hole with three holes inside, so it's clear that, especially in patients with few access vases, it was interesting. It seems strange, but such a simple concept took a long time to be received and we worked exclusively for a while and we were born on this honestly and then slowly we have always looked for others I have always had people rather valid in introducing new techniques we have gone from treating

expensive catheters, but catheters, to the machinery that contains everything. It was something completely new, and what impressed both me and the doctor with whom we went to see it for the first time is how it works. I have a dramatic memory, I was able to make the knots in laparoscopy, which is also difficult for laparoscopists, in half an hour. It is striking how this machine allows for better visibility, it can scale in motion, it eliminates the tremor. It is surprising how much time it took to accept robotic surgery. The system is a technology that is used to perform abdominal surgery mainly in a mini-invasive way.

It is structured by these three components. The surgical vest is basically the operating part that is linked to the patient, it is worn sterilely and on which a particular instrument is mounted and the surgical act must be performed. The video cart, on the other hand, is used to acquire the image. There is a system with a three-dimensional camera that acquires the image from the operating room, transmits it to a monitor that is used by assistants, surgeons and nurses who walk around the room and, above all, creates a three-dimensional image on the surgical console where the surgeon will then sit. The surgical console is the control component, the brain of the machine. It is used by the surgeon to perform the movement and transmit this movement in a more precise and filtered way to the patient. The instrument has an endovrist technology that allows the surgeon to transmit and amplify the movements of the wrist. They have seven degrees of freedom and therefore they can transmit in small spaces

Inside the closed abdomen, the surgeon uses a robotic instrument that mimics traditional open-air techniques in a minimally invasive context. The instrument's tip, controlled by the surgeon's finger movements, features a joint for precise manipulation. The camera, with an eight-millimeter diameter, utilizes two visual channels to acquire a three-dimensional image, which is transmitted to the video cart and surgical console.

What has been done over the years is to try to make both the surgeon at the table and the surgeon at the console more autonomous. The operating component consists of four operating arms, this means that the surgeon can manage both the camera and three operating instruments, which makes it different from any other type of surgery because it manages both the main arms and therefore the operating instruments that perform the surgical side, and a fourth arm or third arm of support that instead manages the tractions and the exposition of the planes. The arms are quite slender, so they allow to be easily positioned on the abdomen and the tools are quite long and therefore this allows you to perform surgery on any type of patient, even obesity surgery if you want. The console is so composed, a three-dimensional stereo viewer, the surgeon rests his head and it's as if he were immersed in the surgical field. He inserts his fingers into what are called remaster controllers, and the movement of the surgeon's fingers is transmitted faithfully on the tip of the instrument.

The system automatically filters the natural tremors of the surgeon's hand and scales the movement up to a maximum of three to one. So the surgeon moves at three, the instrument moves at one, allowing, let's say, a reinforcement of precision. Simply by taking and closing my fingers, the movement of my hands is transferred to the tip of the instrument, you see. I can rotate them, I can open them, I can insert them. Through a pedal I can manage my image, then get closer to what is the operating field. With the side pedal instead I go to manage what is the third operating arm that I can use to take something, leave it there and then continue to go on with the rest. Inside my screen I can also have auxiliary field images, such as an echograph or a diagnosis test that allows the surgeon to have all the information needed to perform the surgery. and from this bracelet he has access to all the information related to the assembly of the system, to the instruments used, to the camera and he can control its use when he wants.

This is one of the models we use to train the surgeons. You take the rings and you make them slide on a scale, trying not to pull them and exploiting all the freedom of movement of the instrument. Normally the instruments that are used in invasive surgery do not have the possibility of articulating the wrist, but they are rigid instruments. As a consequence, what happens is that the surgeon adapts the surgical movement to the instrument, and he sees his technique again ok so what they normally can do is this to rotate it insert it open it and close it and close it sorry instead everything that is this knot they can't they can't articulate it so if to make a knot I do this movement they do this movement they have to The learning curve on this type of system is much faster than the one that is the learning curve in traditional invasive surgery because the movements that can be done are wider.

It seems to be at the top of technology, but it's not like that. Medicine struggles a lot to accept new techniques. The technology of robotics is exceptional. That doesn't take away the fact that, I'll give you an example, it was born in the year two thousand and today, after fifteen years, hardware and software They have improved, but it has nothing to do with how a phone or a car has improved, because always in the medical, the news and the changes go much more slowly than the rest.
